Global AI Job Market & Salary Trends 2025

2026-03-26

#Libraries & Dala Loading

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(dplyr)
library(haven)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(car)       
library(emmeans)  

df<-read.csv("~/Documents/MSc/3ο Trimester/Statistics/ergasia/dataset/ai_job_dataset.csv")

#Prepare Data

#Total observations
n<-nrow(df)
print(paste("Total observations:",n))
## [1] "Total observations: 15000"
#Save original columns
original_columns <- colnames(df)

#Categorizing job titles
df_jobs <- data.frame(
  job_title = c(
    # Data Science & Analytics related jobs
    "Data Scientist", "Data Analyst", "Principal Data Scientist",
    "AI Specialist", "AI Consultant",
    
    # AI Engineering related jobs
    "Machine Learning Engineer", "AI Software Engineer", "Data Engineer", "NLP Engineer",
    "Deep Learning Engineer", "ML Ops Engineer", "Computer Vision Engineer", "Robotics Engineer",
    "Autonomous Systems Engineer",
    
    # Research related jobs
    "AI Research Scientist", "Research Scientist", "Machine Learning Researcher",
    
    # Leadership & Strategy related jobs
    "Head of AI", "AI Architect", "AI Product Manager"
  ),
  job_category = c(
    rep("Data Science & Analytics", 5),
    rep("AI Engineering", 9),
    rep("Research", 3),
    rep("Leadership & Strategy", 3)
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

#Categorizing Regions
df_regions<-data.frame(
  company_location=c(
    #North America
    "United States", "Canada", 
    
    #Europe
    "United Kingdom", "Germany", "France", "Netherlands", "Switzerland", 
    "Denmark", "Norway", "Ireland", "Sweden", "Finland", "Austria",
    
    #Asia
    "China", "India", "Japan", "Singapore", "South Korea", "Israel", 
    
    #Oceania
    "Australia"
  ),
  region=c(
    rep("North America",2),
    rep("Europe",11),
    rep("Asia",6),
    "Oceania"
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

df <- merge(df, df_jobs, by = "job_title", all.x = TRUE)
df <- merge(df, df_regions, by = "company_location", all.x = TRUE)

#Converting to factors
df$job_category <- as.factor(df$job_category)
df$region <- as.factor(df$region)
df$experience_level <- factor(df$experience_level, levels = c("EN", "MI", "SE", "EX"))
df$company_size <- factor(df$company_size, levels = c("S", "M", "L"))

#Keeping the original columns 
df <- df[, c(original_columns, "job_category", "region")]

#Variable control
glimpse(df$salary_usd) 
##  int [1:15000] 151986 60611 64400 170112 93109 96865 181001 52089 157518 66115 ...
summary(df$salary_usd)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   32519   70180   99705  115349  146408  399095
sum(is.na(df$salary_usd))
## [1] 0
# Remove missing salaries if any
df <- df %>% filter(!is.na(salary_usd))

#Visualization and Desctriptive Statistics 

## # A tibble: 4 × 7
##   region            n mean_salary median_salary sd_salary min_salary max_salary
##   <fct>         <int>       <dbl>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <int>      <int>
## 1 Asia           4487      92949.        82885     45167.      32519     306266
## 2 Europe         8288     124572.       108194.    64273.      32542     399095
## 3 North America  1493     130052.       115528     61357.      44119     344471
## 4 Oceania         732     118239.       103206.    54610.      46731     292604

## # A tibble: 3 × 7
##   company_size     n mean_salary median_salary sd_salary min_salary max_salary
##   <fct>        <int>       <dbl>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <int>      <int>
## 1 S             5007     102147.         89648    52245.      32519     322318
## 2 M             4995     113600.         98122    58966.      36066     359597
## 3 L             4998     130322.        114564    65488.      41438     399095

#(ANOVA & TESTS)

# Normality test (Sharip-Wilk) per Region
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, region == "Oceania")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, region == "Oceania")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.90891, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, region == "Asia")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, region == "Asia")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.90766, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, region == "North America")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, region == "North America")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.91239, p-value < 2.2e-16
europe_salary <- na.omit(filter(df, region == "Europe")$salary_usd)
shapiro.test(sample(europe_salary, 5000))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sample(europe_salary, 5000)
## W = 0.9053, p-value < 2.2e-16
#Normality test (Sharip-Wilk) per Job Category
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Data Science & Analytics")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Data Science & Analytics")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.90398, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Research")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Research")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.89354, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Leadership & Strategy")$salary_usd))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  na.omit(filter(df, job_category == "Leadership & Strategy")$salary_usd)
## W = 0.89803, p-value < 2.2e-16
#Taking a random sample of 5,000 observations for AI Engineering
ai_engineering_salary <- na.omit(filter(df,job_category == "AI Engineering")$salary_usd)
shapiro.test(sample(ai_engineering_salary,5000))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sample(ai_engineering_salary, 5000)
## W = 0.90024, p-value < 2.2e-16
#KRUSKAL-WALLIS Test by Job category
kruskal_region1 <- kruskal.test(salary_usd ~ job_category, data = df)
print(kruskal_region1)
## 
##  Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test
## 
## data:  salary_usd by job_category
## Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 2.038, df = 3, p-value = 0.5646
#Post-hoc Test (Pairwise Wilcoxon)
pairwise_region1 <- pairwise.wilcox.test(df$salary_usd, df$job_category, p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
print(pairwise_region1)
## 
##  Pairwise comparisons using Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction 
## 
## data:  df$salary_usd and df$job_category 
## 
##                          AI Engineering Data Science & Analytics
## Data Science & Analytics 1              -                       
## Leadership & Strategy    1              1                       
## Research                 1              1                       
##                          Leadership & Strategy
## Data Science & Analytics -                    
## Leadership & Strategy    -                    
## Research                 1                    
## 
## P value adjustment method: bonferroni
#KRUSKAL-WALLIS Test by Region
kruskal_region2 <- kruskal.test(salary_usd ~ region, data = df)
print(kruskal_region2)
## 
##  Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test
## 
## data:  salary_usd by region
## Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 989.27, df = 3, p-value < 2.2e-16
#Post-hoc Test (Pairwise Wilcoxon)
pairwise_region2 <- pairwise.wilcox.test(df$salary_usd, df$region, p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
print(pairwise_region2)
## 
##  Pairwise comparisons using Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction 
## 
## data:  df$salary_usd and df$region 
## 
##               Asia    Europe  North America
## Europe        < 2e-16 -       -            
## North America < 2e-16 9.8e-05 -            
## Oceania       < 2e-16 0.59    7.9e-05      
## 
## P value adjustment method: bonferroni
#Normality check by company size
#Sharipo-Wilk
shapiro.test(sample(df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "S"], 5000))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sample(df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "S"], 5000)
## W = 0.90552, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "M"])
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "M"]
## W = 0.89557, p-value < 2.2e-16
shapiro.test(df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "L"])
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  df$salary_usd[df$company_size == "L"]
## W = 0.90773, p-value < 2.2e-16
#KRUSKAL-WALLIS test by Company Size
kruskal_size <- kruskal.test(salary_usd ~ company_size, data = df)
print(kruskal_size)
## 
##  Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test
## 
## data:  salary_usd by company_size
## Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 569.22, df = 2, p-value < 2.2e-16
#Post-hoc test (Pairwise Wilcoxon)
pairwise_size <- pairwise.wilcox.test(df$salary_usd, df$company_size, p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
print(pairwise_size)
## 
##  Pairwise comparisons using Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction 
## 
## data:  df$salary_usd and df$company_size 
## 
##   S      M     
## M <2e-16 -     
## L <2e-16 <2e-16
## 
## P value adjustment method: bonferroni
df$log_salary <- log(df$salary_usd)
#Homogenity of variance
leveneTest(log_salary ~ interaction(experience_level, region), data = df)
## Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median)
##          Df F value    Pr(>F)    
## group    15  57.868 < 2.2e-16 ***
##       14984                      
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#Two way ANOVA
fit1 <- aov(log_salary ~ experience_level * region, data = df)
summary(fit1)
##                            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq   F value Pr(>F)    
## experience_level            3 2404.5   801.5 11588.669 <2e-16 ***
## region                      3  242.9    81.0  1170.502 <2e-16 ***
## experience_level:region     9    0.3     0.0     0.523  0.859    
## Residuals               14984 1036.3     0.1                     
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#---Tukey for Experience Level
tukey_results <- as.data.frame(TukeyHSD(fit1, "experience_level")$experience_level)
print(round(tukey_results, 2))
##       diff  lwr  upr p adj
## MI-EN 0.33 0.32 0.35     0
## SE-EN 0.66 0.65 0.68     0
## EX-EN 1.08 1.07 1.10     0
## SE-MI 0.33 0.31 0.34     0
## EX-MI 0.75 0.73 0.77     0
## EX-SE 0.42 0.41 0.44     0
#EMMEANS: Experience within each region
emmeans_results <- emmeans(fit1, pairwise ~ experience_level | region)
print(emmeans_results)
## $emmeans
## region = Asia:
##  experience_level emmean      SE    df lower.CL upper.CL
##  EN                10.81 0.00775 14984    10.80    10.83
##  MI                11.15 0.00792 14984    11.14    11.17
##  SE                11.49 0.00778 14984    11.47    11.50
##  EX                11.90 0.00797 14984    11.88    11.91
## 
## region = Europe:
##  experience_level emmean      SE    df lower.CL upper.CL
##  EN                11.09 0.00588 14984    11.08    11.10
##  MI                11.42 0.00570 14984    11.40    11.43
##  SE                11.75 0.00581 14984    11.73    11.76
##  EX                12.17 0.00573 14984    12.16    12.18
## 
## region = North America:
##  experience_level emmean      SE    df lower.CL upper.CL
##  EN                11.15 0.01390 14984    11.13    11.18
##  MI                11.47 0.01360 14984    11.44    11.50
##  SE                11.81 0.01350 14984    11.78    11.83
##  EX                12.23 0.01350 14984    12.20    12.26
## 
## region = Oceania:
##  experience_level emmean      SE    df lower.CL upper.CL
##  EN                11.09 0.01840 14984    11.05    11.13
##  MI                11.40 0.01980 14984    11.36    11.44
##  SE                11.76 0.02010 14984    11.72    11.80
##  EX                12.15 0.01960 14984    12.11    12.19
## 
## Confidence level used: 0.95 
## 
## $contrasts
## region = Asia:
##  contrast estimate      SE    df  t.ratio p.value
##  EN - MI    -0.340 0.01110 14984  -30.694 <0.0001
##  EN - SE    -0.673 0.01100 14984  -61.252 <0.0001
##  EN - EX    -1.083 0.01110 14984  -97.453 <0.0001
##  MI - SE    -0.332 0.01110 14984  -29.936 <0.0001
##  MI - EX    -0.743 0.01120 14984  -66.134 <0.0001
##  SE - EX    -0.411 0.01110 14984  -36.893 <0.0001
## 
## region = Europe:
##  contrast estimate      SE    df  t.ratio p.value
##  EN - MI    -0.324 0.00819 14984  -39.621 <0.0001
##  EN - SE    -0.654 0.00827 14984  -79.093 <0.0001
##  EN - EX    -1.076 0.00820 14984 -131.099 <0.0001
##  MI - SE    -0.329 0.00814 14984  -40.451 <0.0001
##  MI - EX    -0.751 0.00808 14984  -92.971 <0.0001
##  SE - EX    -0.422 0.00816 14984  -51.701 <0.0001
## 
## region = North America:
##  contrast estimate      SE    df  t.ratio p.value
##  EN - MI    -0.314 0.01940 14984  -16.185 <0.0001
##  EN - SE    -0.654 0.01940 14984  -33.785 <0.0001
##  EN - EX    -1.075 0.01930 14984  -55.568 <0.0001
##  MI - SE    -0.340 0.01920 14984  -17.718 <0.0001
##  MI - EX    -0.761 0.01920 14984  -39.703 <0.0001
##  SE - EX    -0.421 0.01910 14984  -22.047 <0.0001
## 
## region = Oceania:
##  contrast estimate      SE    df  t.ratio p.value
##  EN - MI    -0.307 0.02700 14984  -11.365 <0.0001
##  EN - SE    -0.673 0.02730 14984  -24.680 <0.0001
##  EN - EX    -1.062 0.02690 14984  -39.493 <0.0001
##  MI - SE    -0.366 0.02820 14984  -12.977 <0.0001
##  MI - EX    -0.755 0.02780 14984  -27.124 <0.0001
##  SE - EX    -0.389 0.02810 14984  -13.857 <0.0001
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 4 estimates
#Chi-square test (Region and experience level dependency)
tab <- table(df$region, df$experience_level)
print(tab)
##                
##                   EN   MI   SE   EX
##   Asia          1151 1102 1144 1090
##   Europe        2003 2128 2047 2110
##   North America  360  374  379  380
##   Oceania        204  177  171  180
chi_test <- chisq.test(tab)
chi_test
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  tab
## X-squared = 10.841, df = 9, p-value = 0.2868
